stem: A rule file at .claude/rules/testing.md uses the frontmatter paths: ["tests/**"] so testing conventions apply repository-wide. In practice the rule only reaches files sitting directly in tests/ and skips ones nested deeper, such as tests/unit/user.test.ts. What is the best fix?
options: Relocate the rule into a root-level CLAUDE.md file instead
Change the glob to tests/**/* so the rule reaches nested files recursively
Create a separate rule for every subdirectory beneath tests/
Add prompt guidance describing the situations in which the rule should fire
explanation: The original glob only matches one directory level deep. Appending /* makes the pattern recurse into every nested subdirectory so the rule applies everywhere.

stem: You are refining a data transformation script that has several defects at once: wrong null handling, inconsistent field names, and output that drifts from the expected schema. These problems are interdependent, so fixing one shifts the others. Which refinement approach is most effective?
options: Address each defect in its own separate iteration to keep changes isolated
Describe every defect together in one detailed instruction and iterate a single time
Discard the current code and regenerate the whole implementation from a new prompt
Correct the output format first and only then tackle the remaining defects
explanation: When issues are coupled, handling them together in one pass keeps the solution internally consistent; sequential fixes tend to conflict with or undo earlier changes.

stem: Your CI pipeline uses Claude Code to generate unit tests for new changes, but the output frequently repeats scenarios and overlooks edge cases already handled elsewhere in the suite. You want to raise both relevance and coverage without producing more unnecessary output. Which approach works best?
options: Supply the existing test files as context so Claude can avoid duplication and spot coverage gaps
Cap the number of tests generated per run to cut down on redundancy
Regenerate the tests several times and manually pick the best version
Instruct Claude to only produce test cases that are unique and meaningful
explanation: Giving Claude visibility into the current suite lets it reason about what is already covered and concentrate on the missing scenarios rather than guessing.

stem: You must migrate a legacy monolith into a modular service-based architecture. The work spans dozens of files and requires careful decisions about service boundaries, shared dependencies, and data flow between components. Which approach should you take?
options: Use direct execution with detailed upfront instructions specifying each module's exact structure and responsibilities
Enter plan mode to explore the codebase, map dependencies, and design an approach before making any changes
Begin with direct execution and refactor incrementally so structure emerges as you implement
Start in direct execution and only switch to plan mode if unexpected complexity appears
explanation: Plan mode is built for complex, multi-file work involving architectural decisions and several viable approaches, so exploring and designing first reduces costly rework.

stem: A CI step runs claude "Review this pull request for performance issues" but the job never finishes because logs show Claude Code waiting for interactive input, hanging the pipeline. What is the correct way to run this command in a CI/CD environment?
options: Export an environment variable CLAUDENONINTERACTIVE=true before running the command
Pipe an empty stream in: echo "" | claude "Review this pull request for performance issues"
Pass a --cl flag: claude --cl "Review this pull request for performance issues"
Add the -p flag: claude -p "Review this pull request for performance issues"
explanation: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code non-interactively: it processes the prompt, prints the result, and exits immediately instead of waiting for input.

stem: You are using Claude to generate validation logic for a form processing system. The first version handles basic cases but breaks on edge cases like null values and invalid formats. You want to systematically improve it while guaranteeing correctness across all scenarios. Which approach is most effective?
options: Add more written instructions describing how each edge case should be handled
Give a high-level description of every possible edge case in the prompt
Write test cases covering expected behavior and edge cases, then iterate by feeding back failing results
Regenerate the implementation repeatedly until it happens to produce the right behavior
explanation: Test-driven iteration supplies concrete, verifiable feedback that steers the model toward correct behavior far more reliably than prose descriptions or blind regeneration.

stem: Your team builds a skill that performs bulk updates across many files. It emits verbose output during execution and could touch critical parts of the codebase. Your single most important requirement is that the skill must not modify anything outside a safe set of file operations. Which configuration best enforces that boundary?
options: Configure allowed-tools to restrict the skill to a specific set of file-modification tools
Add prompt instructions telling the skill to avoid risky operations
Place the skill logic inside CLAUDE.md so it runs automatically
Add a post-run script that reverts any unexpected file changes
explanation: Restricting allowed-tools enforces a hard safety boundary at the permission layer, guaranteeing the skill can only invoke approved file operations rather than relying on the model to behave.

stem: Your CI pipeline runs Claude Code to review pull requests and post inline comments. When developers push follow-up commits the review re-runs, and you notice Claude keeps reporting the same issues from earlier runs, creating duplicate comments and noise. What is the most effective way to improve this workflow?
options: Include the prior review findings in the new run's context and instruct Claude to report only new or unresolved issues
Add prompt instructions asking Claude not to repeat itself when reviewing updated pull requests
Run Claude in a fresh session each time so earlier comments cannot influence the next run
Filter out duplicate comments after the run by matching repeated text in the CI system
explanation: Supplying prior findings gives Claude the context to distinguish already-reported issues from newly introduced or still-unresolved ones, eliminating duplicate comments at the source.

stem: Your team's coding standards live in a CLAUDE.md at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, and on your machine Claude follows them consistently. When a new teammate clones the repository, Claude does not apply the same standards for them. What is the most likely cause?
options: The configuration is defined at the user level and is therefore not shared through version control
The CLAUDE.md file must be referenced with @import syntax to take effect
The CLAUDE.md file is missing required frontmatter configuration
The repository lacks enough example files for Claude to learn the conventions
explanation: Files under ~/.claude/ apply only to the local user and are never committed, so project-level standards must live in the repository to reach every teammate.

stem: Two developers run the same command on the same repository but observe different behavior from Claude: one gets the testing conventions applied correctly and the other does not. Both confirm the .claude/CLAUDE.md file is present in the repo. What is the most effective way to diagnose the issue?
options: Consolidate all configuration into a single centralized file
Re-run the command several times to check whether the behavior is consistent
Add more detailed instructions to the CLAUDE.md file
Use the /memory command to inspect which configuration files are loaded in each session
explanation: The /memory command reveals which configuration sources are actually active in a session, exposing missing imports, scope problems, or inconsistent loading between the two developers.

stem: Your team creates a skill that performs deep codebase analysis and produces a large volume of intermediate output. After it runs, subsequent interactions behave inconsistently because the main conversation context is filled with verbose analysis data. Which change best addresses this?
options: Move the skill logic into CLAUDE.md so it runs automatically
Add prompt instructions telling the skill to reduce its output verbosity
Enable context: fork so the skill runs in an isolated sub-agent context
Split the skill into several smaller commands
explanation: A forked context isolates the skill's execution so its large output stays out of the main conversation, keeping later interactions clean and predictable.

stem: Your project has a large CLAUDE.md containing many different standards at once: API conventions, testing guidelines, and deployment rules. Different parts of the codebase need different subsets of these. You want to modularize the configuration so only the relevant rules load in each context. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Move all standards into user-level configuration to simplify the project files
Use .claude/rules/ with path-based scoping so only the relevant conventions load per file
Duplicate smaller CLAUDE.md files into each directory containing only its relevant rules
Keep everything in one CLAUDE.md and trust the model to apply the correct sections
explanation: Path-scoped rules selectively load conventions based on file context, cutting irrelevant instructions and improving model performance without duplicating configuration everywhere.

stem: You must migrate a large codebase to a new logging library. The change touches many modules and requires choosing among several integration approaches; once that approach is chosen the implementation steps are straightforward. What is the most appropriate workflow?
options: Perform the entire task in plan mode to keep everything consistent
Use plan mode first to select the approach, then switch to direct execution for the implementation
Start directly in execution mode since the changes are well-defined
Alternate between plan mode and execution after every single step
explanation: Plan mode is best for weighing the architectural decision, while direct execution is efficient for applying the now-known changes, so combining them fits this two-phase task.

stem: You ask Claude to implement a caching layer for an API. The requirements are high-level and involve considerations like cache invalidation, expiration policies, and failure handling. Initial attempts produce incomplete implementations that miss important design considerations. What is the most effective next step?
options: Write test cases immediately and iterate based on the failures
Regenerate the implementation multiple times using the same prompt
Use an interview-style approach where Claude asks clarifying questions before implementing
Provide much more detailed instructions describing every caching scenario
explanation: When requirements are ambiguous and involve multiple design tradeoffs, the interview pattern surfaces missing constraints and decisions before any code is written.

stem: You are fixing a bug in a single function based on a clear stack trace. The issue is isolated and the required change is straightforward. A developer suggests using plan mode first to explore the codebase and weigh multiple approaches before making any modifications. What is the best course of action?
options: Proceed with direct execution since the task is well-scoped and clearly understood
Use plan mode to make sure all possible approaches are considered before changing anything
Start in plan mode and switch to execution after reviewing the function
Use plan mode to confirm there are no hidden dependencies
explanation: Direct execution suits simple, well-defined changes; invoking plan mode for an isolated one-function fix only adds unnecessary overhead.

stem: Your codebase has areas with different conventions (frontend, backend, data access), and test files are scattered throughout, living next to the files they test (e.g. user.service.test.ts beside user.service.ts). You want every test file to follow the same conventions no matter where it lives. What is the most maintainable way to make Claude apply the correct conventions automatically?
options: Create rule files in .claude/rules/ using YAML frontmatter with glob patterns that apply conventions based on file paths
Define all conventions in the root CLAUDE.md and rely on Claude to infer which rules apply in each context
Create separate skills in .claude/skills/ for each code type and embed conventions in each skill definition
Add a CLAUDE.md to every directory with localized rules specific to that part of the codebase
explanation: Glob-based rule files apply conventions automatically by matching file paths, so consistently named test files inherit the same rules regardless of which directory they sit in.

stem: A platform team keeps org-wide standards in a root-level .claude/CLAUDE.md. During a production outage, infrastructure teams added directory-scoped CLAUDE.md files holding temporary emergency deployment overrides. Months later, unrelated workflows in those directories still follow the emergency behavior even though the incident is resolved. Which governance change prevents this persistent drift while still allowing emergency overrides when truly needed?
options: Store all emergency operational procedures permanently in repository-scoped configuration so future incidents can reuse them automatically
Eliminate directory-scoped CLAUDE.md files entirely and force every workflow to inherit only root-level policy
Increase the context window so Claude can distinguish temporary instructions from long-term standards automatically
Move temporary incident-specific overrides into short-lived task context or explicitly versioned operational workflows instead of persistent scoped configuration
explanation: Temporary overrides belong in ephemeral task context or versioned workflows, not persistent configuration files, so they expire naturally instead of silently governing unrelated future work.

stem: An engineer asks Claude Code to investigate a distributed caching issue across several services. Early exploration reveals unexpected interactions between cache invalidation, async event handlers, and deployment timing. Instead of following a fixed sequence, Claude dynamically shifts strategy, tracing event propagation, spawning targeted dependency searches, and prioritizing recently modified modules, and finds the root cause far faster. Which principle BEST explains why this adaptive exploration improves investigation quality?
options: Claude performs better when exploration steps are deliberately unstructured and randomized throughout an investigation
Reliability improves mainly because adaptive workflows maximize total context usage during execution
Dynamic exploration works chiefly because recently modified files are always the cause of distributed failures
Dynamic investigation refinement lets Claude adapt exploration depth and direction based on newly discovered evidence
explanation: Adaptive investigation succeeds because Claude continuously redirects its exploration in response to fresh evidence, rather than because of randomness, context volume, or a fixed heuristic about recent files.

stem: A large monorepo holds separate domains for frontend apps, infrastructure automation, and regulated financial services. The platform team maintains shared standards in a root-level .claude/CLAUDE.md while domain teams add directory-scoped CLAUDE.md files. Engineers find that infrastructure workflows sometimes inherit frontend-oriented testing and formatting guidance that conflicts with deployment validation. Which configuration strategy improves correctness while staying maintainable?
options: Remove all domain-level CLAUDE.md files and rely entirely on user prompts during operational workflows
Use layered scoped configurations where shared global standards stay centralized while domain-specific workflows are isolated within their relevant directory boundaries
Move all domain-specific guidance into the root-level configuration so every workflow shares identical behavior
Increase the context window so Claude can reconcile conflicting instructions across unrelated domains automatically
explanation: Layered scoping keeps shared standards central while confining domain-specific rules to their own directories, preventing one domain's guidance from bleeding into another.

stem: A large engineering org uses shared @import-based configuration files to distribute standards across hundreds of repositories. Over time, some imported modules become deprecated after policy changes, yet many repos keep relying on the outdated imports because engineers do not know newer modules should replace them. Which governance approach best improves long-term configuration reliability and maintainability?
options: Introduce versioned imported configuration modules with explicit deprecation and migration guidance workflows
Replace modular imports with fully duplicated repository-level instructions to remove dependency-management complexity
Allow repositories to keep using deprecated imports indefinitely so existing workflows remain stable
Increase the context window so Claude can automatically infer which imported configurations are outdated during execution
explanation: Versioning imported modules with clear deprecation and migration guidance lets teams see what is outdated and how to upgrade, keeping shared configuration reliable at scale.

stem: A platform team builds reusable Claude Code skills for dependency analysis, rollout validation, drift detection, and incident summarization. Engineers currently invoke them manually in different sequences per scenario. The org wants workflows to compose these skills dynamically based on runtime investigation signals while preserving consistency and maintainability. Which architectural approach best supports this?
options: Keep the skills modular and orchestrate them dynamically using structured runtime decision logic
Duplicate commonly reused operational logic across every skill to minimize inter-skill dependencies
Replace the reusable skills with repository-level CLAUDE.md instructions so workflows stay globally accessible
Merge all operational skills into one centralized workflow containing every possible execution path
explanation: Keeping skills modular and composing them through explicit runtime decision logic preserves reuse and maintainability while allowing dynamic, signal-driven orchestration.

stem: A reliability team uses Claude Code to investigate a complex cross-service latency issue, accumulating dependency maps, validated findings, disproven theories, and infrastructure observations over several hours. The investigation must then hand off to another team in a different time zone, who historically spend significant effort reconstructing context and sometimes revisit already-disproven hypotheses. Which workflow adjustment best improves investigation continuity across the handoff?
options: Export the complete conversation transcript and require the receiving team to review the entire history before continuing
Create structured investigation artifacts summarizing validated findings, active hypotheses, rejected theories, and next-step recommendations
Restart the investigation from first principles so the receiving team forms unbiased independent conclusions
Increase the context window so future sessions can automatically include all historical investigation activity
explanation: Structured artifacts capture what is known, what is still open, and what was ruled out, letting the receiving team resume efficiently without re-deriving context or repeating dead ends.

stem: A large engineering org maintains shared coding standards, security requirements, and deployment procedures across many repositories. Teams first duplicated these across many separate CLAUDE.md files, but keeping them consistent grew harder as standards evolved, so they adopt the @import syntax inside CLAUDE.md. Which benefit BEST explains why this improves long-term maintainability?
options: @import increases the effective context window by automatically compressing imported content
@import automatically merges all repository configurations into one global execution context regardless of scope
@import lets external markdown files be centrally maintained and reused across many Claude Code configurations
@import prevents repository-level configuration overrides so all teams inherit identical behavior permanently
explanation: @import references shared markdown files that are maintained in one place and reused everywhere, so a single edit propagates to every configuration instead of requiring duplicate updates.

stem: A Claude-based code review pipeline runs four validation stages (security, architecture, performance, and style) sequentially, and total execution time has grown too long. You need to reduce overall pipeline runtime while keeping validation accuracy intact. Which workflow design best achieves this?
options: Spawn independent subagents for each validation component and run them concurrently, then aggregate their results in a coordinating agent once all complete
Combine all four validation components into a single agent prompt and rely on the model to internally parallelize the analysis
Cache the results of previous pipeline runs and skip components whose inputs have not changed since the last execution
Run the validation components sequentially but shrink the scope of each one to reduce its individual execution time
explanation: Independent subagents run the four validations in parallel and a coordinator merges their outputs, cutting wall-clock time while each component keeps its full analytical scope and accuracy.

stem: You are analyzing a large legacy codebase to understand dependencies before a refactor. The discovery phase produces extensive output, including file relationships, module usage, and configuration patterns. In testing you notice that after this analysis, later steps become less accurate and the model starts missing important details. What is the most appropriate improvement?
options: Use the Explore subagent to isolate the discovery output and return a concise summary
Add prompt instructions asking the model to be more concise
Reduce the amount of analysis performed before starting implementation
Switch directly to execution mode to avoid producing large outputs
explanation: The Explore subagent keeps bulky intermediate discovery output out of the main context and returns only a concise summary, preserving clarity and accuracy in the steps that follow.

stem: An engineer uses Claude Code to debug an intermittent data corruption issue in a distributed pipeline. Two competing theories emerge, one about serialization inconsistencies and another about async retry ordering. The engineer wants to pursue both independently while ensuring that assumptions and reasoning from one path do not unintentionally bias the other. Which workflow best supports this?
options: Merge all evidence from both theories into a shared summary document before continuing exploration sequentially
Restart the investigation from scratch for each theory to eliminate all prior contextual influence completely
Fork the investigation into isolated exploration branches that inherit shared baseline context but keep independent reasoning histories
Continue exploring both theories inside a single continuously evolving reasoning chain so all evidence stays globally visible
explanation: Forking into isolated branches lets each theory start from the same baseline yet reason independently, preventing cross-contamination without discarding the shared context.

stem: A Claude-based deployment orchestration workflow packs system policies, rollback procedures, compliance requirements, incident history, infrastructure metadata, and runtime diagnostics into one prompt before generating remediation recommendations. During incidents, the model occasionally prioritizes detailed diagnostic logs over critical deployment safety constraints defined earlier. Which configuration adjustment best ensures higher-priority organizational constraints get appropriate attention relative to lower-priority guidance?
options: Compress all infrastructure diagnostics aggressively so the prompt holds only high-level summaries during remediation
Increase temperature so Claude balances operational diagnostics and deployment constraints more dynamically
Increase the context window so all operational information stays simultaneously visible during orchestration
Reassert critical deployment constraints near the decision-generation stages while structurally separating operational data from authoritative instructions
explanation: Placing critical constraints close to where decisions are made and separating raw data from authoritative instructions ensures high-priority policies are not drowned out by voluminous diagnostics.

stem: An engineering team uses Claude Code to investigate production failures in a large distributed codebase. Some engineers immediately ask Claude to modify files after a brief description, while others first run exploration-oriented workflows using Grep, Glob, dependency tracing, and planning before requesting changes. The second group consistently produces more reliable fixes with fewer regressions. Which explanation BEST describes why exploration-first improves reliability?
options: Exploration workflows let Claude gather architectural context and dependency relationships before making implementation decisions
Exploration workflows mainly improve reliability because they increase context window usage and expose more source code at once
Immediate implementation requests reduce reliability because Claude cannot modify files until all repository files are read completely
Claude performs best when implementation tasks are delayed because reasoning quality improves automatically over time
explanation: Exploring first builds an accurate picture of architecture and dependencies, so the resulting changes account for real relationships and avoid the regressions that blind edits cause.

stem: An engineer uses Claude Code to investigate a cascading timeout issue in a distributed event-processing platform. Early investigation surfaces dozens of potentially related services, retry handlers, and async message flows, and Claude initially tries to explore every dependency path in parallel, causing context growth and reduced precision. The engineer wants Claude to stay adaptive while avoiding uncontrolled exploration. Which workflow adjustment best improves investigation quality?
options: Continue exploring all dependency paths simultaneously so no potentially relevant signal is missed
Restrict exploration to only the directly failing services regardless of observed downstream interactions
Increase the context window so all explored dependencies stay permanently visible during debugging
Introduce iterative prioritization checkpoints that continuously rank investigation paths by newly discovered evidence and diagnostic value
explanation: Periodic prioritization checkpoints keep the investigation focused on the highest-value paths as evidence accumulates, preserving adaptivity without letting exploration sprawl and dilute precision.

stem: Your team creates a skill that runs a multi-step analysis: scan files, identify issues, then propose fixes. It is configured with context: fork to isolate execution. In testing, the final output is incomplete: the skill identifies issues but fails to propose fixes based on the earlier findings. What is the most likely cause?
options: The skill should be split into multiple separate commands instead of one workflow
The CLAUDE.md file is missing required configuration for multi-step tasks
The skill needs more detailed prompt instructions to complete all steps
The forked context prevents earlier results from being available across the later steps
explanation: context: fork isolates execution, which breaks workflows that depend on accumulating intermediate state, so the later fix-proposal step cannot see the findings from the earlier steps.
